narration.default: Picture a little robot, no remote, no driver — just rolling along a black line painted on the floor. It steers around curves, stays on track, and never falls off. Today you'll teach one to do exactly that. Two eyes underneath, two motors, and a handful of decisions in code. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you've finished the earlier tutorials in this series, you've already met every block we'll use today — we're just putting them together in a new shape.

Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You know the drill by now — setup runs once when the program starts, loop runs forever after that. Our robot's brain lives inside loop: read the eyes, decide which way to go, drive the motors, repeat — many times every second.

Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 2 and mode to input pullup. Pin 2 is the robot's left eye — it'll listen for what the sensor underneath the robot is reporting. input pullup keeps the reading stable, the same way it did for the button in the earlier tutorial.

Quick puzzle. The robot has no camera. It has no eyeballs. So how does it actually see the line on the floor? It can't take a photo, it can't recognise shapes — and yet somehow it knows which way to turn. Hold that question for a moment. The answer is simpler than you'd guess, and it's coming up next.

steps.title: Two IR sensors that read brightness

narration.default: Here's the answer. The robot has two tiny IR sensors pointing down at the floor. Each one shines invisible infrared light at the ground and reads how much bounces back. Black floor absorbs the light — almost nothing bounces back, so the sensor reads low. White floor reflects the light — lots bounces back, so the sensor reads high. That's it. No camera, no AI — just two sensors saying bright or dark, many times every second. The Arduino reads those two numbers and decides where to steer.

Drag another pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 3 and mode to input pullup. Pin 3 is the right eye. Two sensors, two pins — the robot will compare what each one reports and steer based on the difference.

Drag a third pinMode. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 is the left motor. We picked pin 9 because it's a PWM pin — that lets us control motor speed, not just on or off. You met PWM in the LED glow tutorial; same idea here, but driving a motor instead of fading a light.
One more pinMode. Set pin to 10 and mode to output. Pin 10 is the right motor — also a PWM pin. Two eyes listening on pins 2 and 3, two motors talking on pins 9 and 10. Setup is done.

A quick heads-up about the simulator. Real motors need a special chip called a motor driver — the Arduino can't power them directly. To keep the focus on the programming and not on wiring chips, the simulator swaps the motors for LEDs. Bright LED means the motor is spinning fast. Dim LED means it's slowing down. Dark LED means the motor is stopped. The code you write today is exactly the same code that would drive a real two-wheeled robot — only the components in the simulator are swapped.

Drag an analogWrite block into the top of the loop. Set pin to 9, value to 200. analogWrite sends a number from 0 to 255 — zero is stopped, 255 is full speed. We're picking 200, which is a nice cruising speed. This sets the left motor running every loop, before any decisions get made.

steps.title: Right motor at the same speed

narration.default: Drag a second analogWrite right below the first. Pin 10, value 200. Both motors running at speed 200 — left and right matched. When both wheels spin the same speed, the robot rolls straight ahead. Now we'll add the steering logic underneath.

Open Control and drag a plain if block below the two analogWrites. Then build its diamond slot the way you've done before — drop an equals block in, put digitalRead pin 2 on the left, and a low block on the right. The question reads: is the left eye seeing the line right now?

steps.title: Left eye low means steer left

narration.default: Pause for a moment. If the left eye is reading low, that means it's sitting over the dark line — and the line has drifted to the left side of the robot. So the robot needs to steer left to follow it. We do that by stopping just the left motor; the right motor keeps spinning, and the robot pivots around its left wheel. The next step writes that into code.

steps.title: Stop the left motor to turn left

narration.default: Drag an analogWrite into the if branch — the slot inside the if block. Pin 9, value 0. That stops the left motor. The right motor is still spinning at 200, so the robot pivots: right wheel pushing forward, left wheel still — the robot rotates to the left, exactly what we want when the left eye spots the line.

Drag a second if block below the first. Build its diamond the same way — equals, digitalRead pin 3 on the left, low on the right. This question is the mirror image of the left-eye one: is the right eye seeing the line right now?

steps.title: Right eye low means steer right

narration.default: Same shape as before, mirrored. If the right eye is reading low, the line has drifted to the right side of the robot — so we steer right by stopping the right motor. Left motor keeps spinning, robot pivots around its right wheel. Next step writes that.

steps.title: Stop the right motor to turn right

narration.default: Drag an analogWrite into the second if branch. Pin 10, value 0. Right motor stops, left motor keeps spinning — robot pivots to the right. Two ifs, mirror images of each other. Each loop: drive both motors at 200, then check each eye and stop the matching motor if that eye sees the line. That's the entire steering brain.

Drag a delay block at the very bottom of the loop. Set it to 50 milliseconds. That's a tiny pause — twenty times per second, the robot reads the eyes, picks a direction, and adjusts the motors. Fast enough to follow curves, slow enough not to peg the Arduino. Every loop should have at least a small delay.

steps.title: Add the left sensor on pin 2

narration.default: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose Push Button. Set pin to 2 and click Save. The simulator doesn't have an actual IR sensor component, so we use a push button as a stand-in for the left eye. Pressing the button is the same as the eye seeing the black line — it pulls pin 2 low. Not pressing means the eye is over white floor.

steps.title: Add the right sensor on pin 3

narration.default: Click the plus button again and choose Push Button. Set pin to 3, click Save. That's the right eye — same trick, different pin. Two buttons in the simulator standing in for two IR sensors on the real robot.

steps.title: Add the left motor on pin 9

narration.default: Click the plus button and choose LED. Pin 9, Save. This LED is the left motor stand-in. Bright glow means the motor is running at speed 200. Dark means the motor is stopped — robot turning.

steps.title: Add the right motor on pin 10

narration.default: One more LED — plus button, choose LED, pin 10, Save. Right motor stand-in. Two buttons for the eyes, two LEDs for the motors. The whole robot is on screen.
Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.

Press neither button. Both LEDs glow at the same brightness — the robot is driving straight. Now press and hold the left button. The left LED goes dark while the right one stays glowing — the left motor stopped, the right motor kept spinning, so the robot is turning left. Let go, press the right button — right LED goes dark, robot turns right. You just built a line follower. Real robots scale this up — a third eye in the centre for sharper turns, a camera to spot intersections, a distance sensor to brake before obstacles. The same shape every time: sense, decide, drive. Five minutes ago you saw a black line on a floor and wondered how a robot could possibly follow it. Now you've written the brain that does it. Two sensors, two motors, two if blocks, and one delay — that's a self-driving robot.
